question: What are current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents?

heuristic_evaluation.question: What are current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents?

claim_lines: Current approaches to evaluating tool use in LLM agents cluster into five practical layers: (1) **task success** on realistic or executable benchmarks, (2) **tool-call correctness** via trace- or step-level checks, (3) **argument validity** via parameter/data-precision scoring, (4) **recovery from tool errors** via perturbation and fault-injection stress tests, and (5) **cost and latency** tracking for production readiness. The literature is moving from single-run success rates toward more realistic, fine-grained, and operationally relevant evaluation. `https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.16416` `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/trace-grading` ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.16416))

- **task success** — Benchmarks like GTA emphasize end-to-end completion on real-world tasks with real deployed tools and multimodal inputs; the survey also notes a shift toward more realistic benchmarks. `https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/hash/8a75ee6d4b2eb0b777f549a32a5a5c28-Abstract-Datasets_and_Benchmarks_Track.html` ([proceedings.neurips.cc](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper_files/paper/2024/hash/8a75ee6d4b2eb0b777f549a32a5a5c28-Abstract-Datasets_and_Benchmarks_Track.html))

- **tool-call correctness** — Current framework docs grade traces with structured criteria and explicitly ask whether the agent selected and invoked the right tool. `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/trace-grading` `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices` ([platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/trace-grading))

- **argument validity** — A common pattern is `data precision`: verifying the tool is called with the correct arguments extracted from context. `https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices` ([platform.openai.com](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices))

- **recovery from tool errors** — Newer benchmarks inject timeouts, rate limits, partial responses, schema drift, and other failures to measure fault tolerance and recovery. `https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.06112` ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.06112))

- **cost and latency** — Operational evaluation frameworks increasingly log per-tool-call latency, token usage, and USD cost; the survey notes cost-efficiency remains an open gap. `https://iris-eval.com/` `https://arxiv.org/abs/2503.16416` ([iris-eval.com](https://www.iris-eval.com/))

coverage_audit.reason: Clearly addresses end-to-end task completion on realistic/executable benchmarks.
Explicitly covers trace/step-level checks for selecting and invoking the right tool.
Directly names parameter/data-precision scoring for validating tool arguments.
Covers perturbation and fault-injection stress tests for failures like timeouts and schema drift.
Explicitly discusses per-call latency, token usage, and USD cost tracking.

citation_audit.claim: Current approaches cluster into task success, tool-call correctness, argument validity, recovery from tool errors, and cost/latency.
citation_audit.reason: The survey and trace-grading doc plausibly support the high-level framing, though not necessarily the exact five-layer taxonomy.

citation_audit.claim: Benchmarks like GTA emphasize end-to-end completion on real-world tasks with real deployed tools and multimodal inputs.
citation_audit.reason: The benchmark source is directly relevant to task success and realistic tool-use evaluation.

citation_audit.claim: Trace grading docs explicitly ask whether the agent selected and invoked the right tool.
citation_audit.reason: Provider evaluation docs are an appropriate source type for tool-call correctness.

citation_audit.claim: Argument validity is commonly evaluated as data precision: verifying correct arguments extracted from context.
citation_audit.reason: This is a plausible and relevant framework claim, though it is somewhat specialized terminology.

citation_audit.claim: Newer benchmarks inject timeouts, rate limits, partial responses, schema drift, and other failures to measure recovery.
citation_audit.reason: A reliability/stress-condition benchmark is directly aligned with this claim.

citation_audit.claim: Operational evaluation frameworks increasingly log per-tool-call latency, token usage, and USD cost.
citation_audit.reason: The source is relevant but the answer does not establish, from the citation metadata alone, that it specifically supports this detailed logging claim.

source_audit.reason: Relevant survey source for current evaluation approaches.
Direct benchmark source for task success and realistic tool-use evaluation.
Relevant provider documentation for trace-level evaluation of tool use.
Relevant framework documentation, especially for argument validity and grading practices.
Relevant benchmark-style source for robustness and recovery under failures.
Potentially relevant, but as a standalone site it is less established and the answer relies on it for cost/latency claims.

Appears in the source list but is not used substantively and is not clearly relevant to the question.

judge_evaluation.summary: The answer covers all required aspects well and is grounded in relevant benchmark/survey/docs sources, but evidence quality is dragged down by weaker sources and a thin limitations section.
judge_evaluation.recommended_fix: Replace weak or peripheral sources with more direct benchmark/survey citations for cost, latency, and robustness, and add a brief caveat distinguishing what is standardized today versus still fragmented.
